20200709-subject-01/20200709_141754 (63 frames, same as 2026-04-17 grouped ref)
Goal
Determine whether the multi-seq performance gap vs. the tight 30–34 overfit is explained by sequence scope alone, under an apples-to-apples setup (same bs, same L1 loss, same GPU count) made possible by the pre-FPS refactor.

TL;DR

The grouped-sampler constraint was an implementation artifact. Moving FPS sampling into the dataset — producing fixed [N_o=256, 3] object_query_points per sample — lets batches mix objects freely. exp_a (1 sequence, bs=4 via oversample×256) and exp_b (full subject-01 100 sequences, bs=4, no grouping) now share the same architecture, batch geometry, optimizer, and L1 hand loss. exp_a is still dramatically better: hand L2 mean 11.84 mm vs. 21.46 mm, object L2 mean 9.19 mm vs. 50.07 mm. With all other variables held constant, widening training from 1 sequence to 100 sequences (same subject) degrades hand reconstruction ~2× and object reconstruction ~5× in the same 2000 steps — confirming multi-seq training itself is the regression, not the sampler or loss choice.

Pre-FPS Refactor

Prior multi-seq training required ObjectGroupedBatchSampler: every batch had to contain a single YCB object so DCFBranch.prepare_extra_global_tokens could run one FPS on object_vertices_canonical[0] and re-use the indices across the batch. That limited batch_size to the per-object sample count (5 on subject-01) and made 1-sequence × bs>1 geometrically infeasible.

The fix moves the object FPS into the dataset: DexYCBDCFDataset.__getitem__ now returns object_query_points: [N_o, 3] alongside the full object_vertices_canonical. Because N_o=256 is constant across YCB objects, dcf_collate_fn stacks these to [B, 256, 3] without caring about V_o. DCFBranch.prepare_extra_global_tokens and STream3R.forward now accept object_query_points and route around the old FPS-on-batch-[0] path. A new --oversample-manifest N flag repeats the manifest in-memory so a single-sequence overfit can reach arbitrary batch sizes by re-sampling different random frame windows.

Files touched in 8700315

  • stream3r/data/dexycb_dcf_dataset.py — emit object_query_points, add key to _STACKABLE_KEYS
  • stream3r/models/dcf_branch.py — accept object_query_points, prefer it over FPS-on-[0]
  • stream3r/models/stream3r.py — plumb object_query_points through forward
  • scripts/dcf_dataloader_train.py — pass batch's object_query_points to model; add --oversample-manifest
  • scripts/dcf_memory_probe.py — new (single-GPU peak-memory sweep used to size the ACP jobs; hit sampler StopIteration at bs=6 on subject-01 with drop_last, confirming the 5-samples-per-object bound)

Smoke test on the debug container: a plain (non-grouped) 4-sample batch drawn from subject-01 ran one full forward+backward with loss=1.373 and per-item B=4 accounting — proof mixed-object batching works end-to-end. Subsequent batches failed with NVML_SUCCESS assertion errors, a known debug-container GPU-allocator quirk that does not appear on ACP nodes.

Both runs evaluate on the same sequence (20200709-subject-01/20200709_141754, 63 frames). exp_a trains on this sequence and is a memorization ceiling; exp_b adds 99 more subject-01 sequences and is the fair multi-seq comparison. The chamfer ≈ L2 on exp_a means points match in both location and identity; the >50 mm obj L2 on exp_b with chamfer only 1.22× higher says exp_b's object predictions are displaced as a rigid group — not misarticulated, just mis-placed.

Discussion

Multi-sequence training itself is the regression. Under identical architecture (pre-FPS, GPS, per-view), identical optimizer, identical batch geometry, identical L1-only loss, and identical 2000-step budget, expanding the training set from 1 sequence to 100 sequences (same subject, same camera, same hand shape) doubles hand error and quintuples object error on the shared eval sequence. The gap cannot be explained by the object-grouped-sampler constraint, chamfer-vs-L1 confound, bs mismatch, or GPU-count imbalance — all of those were equalized by the pre-FPS refactor.

Where does the capacity go? The exp_a training hand L1 is already <0.01 mm at 2000 steps (clear memorization signature). exp_b's training loss sits around 0.012–0.018 mm with high step-to-step variance, suggesting the network spends capacity averaging across different grasps / objects / timings rather than fitting any single sequence tightly. The object head suffers most because object identity is per-sequence: 20 different YCB objects each need their own pose trajectory, whereas the hand is shared across all 100 sequences.

The chamfer ≈ L2 split tells us the failure mode. On exp_a the two are nearly equal (14.72 vs. 11.84 on hand; 14.37 vs. 9.19 on object) — predictions are on the right cloud, just slightly displaced. On exp_b the hand ratio is 1.02 (same behavior) but the object ratio is 1.22 — exp_b's object predictions are still on the right cluster, but the whole cluster sits 50 mm off. That is the signature of an under-specified object-pose prior being averaged across objects.

Next Steps

  • Longer exp_b: re-run at ≥ 8000 steps to check whether the gap closes with more epochs, or whether exp_b plateaus above exp_a. 100 sequences × 100 steps each would be roughly symmetric with exp_a's 2000 × 1-sequence.
  • Per-object object-pose conditioning: if the object gap is "averaging across objects", a per-object embedding or explicit 6DoF pose target should close it without hurting the hand.
  • Scale-invariant / object-local loss: the 50 mm exp_b object offset is a rigid displacement. An object-frame loss (transform pred/GT into the canonical mesh's frame before L1) would be more informative than a world-frame L2.
  • Cross-subject baseline: rerun exp_b with subject-01 + subject-02 to check if subject-shape mixing adds to the hand regression.

Artifacts live under /mnt/afs/haonan/hoi3r/HOI3R/.worktrees/feat-dcf/logs/dcf_exp_a_1seq_4gpu_bs4_l1/ and …/dcf_exp_b_subject01_4gpu_bs4_l1/. Per-step history, step-500 / 1000 / 1500 / 2000 checkpoints, predictions_subject01.pt, camera-view PNG stacks, and final dcf_dataloader_final.pt are all preserved for follow-up inference.
